planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade; Carolina silverbell is one of the finest native small trees for understory planting under tall deciduous trees.

directions: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly to moderately acidic soil in partial to dappled shade; Carolina silverbell performs best in the cool, moist conditions of forest understory and does not tolerate drought or heavy clay well. Halesia carolina (syn. H. tetraptera) is a beautiful native small tree of the southern Appalachians producing cascading clusters of pure-white, bell-shaped, 4-petaled flowers covering the bare branches in early spring before the leaves emerge, creating a breathtaking floral display; the flowers are followed by ornamental, 4-winged, tan seed pods that persist through fall. One of the most spectacular spring-flowering native trees of the Southeast, excellent as a specimen or in mass plantings along woodland paths; the flowers are particularly lovely when viewed from below against a blue sky. Outstanding companion for dogwood, redbud, and rhododendron.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; slender deutzia is an adaptable, low-maintenance shrub for borders and foundations.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; deutzia blooms on old wood, so prune immediately after flowering in late spring by removing older canes at the base to renew the plant. Deutzia gracilis is a Japanese native deciduous shrub producing fountains of arching stems smothered in late spring with pure-white, star-shaped, 5-petaled flowers in upright, cylindrical racemes — one of the most reliably and abundantly flowering shrubs for temperate gardens. The slender, arching habit and prolific flowering make it ideal as a specimen, in groups, or as a flowering hedge in zones 4–8; the compact cultivar 'Nikko' (12–18 inches) is particularly useful as a groundcover or edging plant and offers outstanding red-purple fall color.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Lewis's mock orange is the state flower of Idaho and one of the most cold-hardy and fragrant native mock oranges.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Lewis's mock orange is drought-tolerant once established and adapts to a wide range of soils including rocky slopes and thin, dry soils. Philadelphus lewisii is a native western North American shrub (state flower of Idaho) producing clusters of large, 4-petaled, pure-white flowers with a pronounced, sweet, citrus-orange fragrance in late spring and early summer — one of the most powerfully perfumed of all flowering shrubs. Unlike the commonly cultivated European species (P. coronarius), this native is more drougt-tolerant, cold-hardy, and has excellent wildlife value; it provides important cover and nesting habitat for birds, and the fragrant flowers attract butterflies and native bees. The multi-stemmed, arching habit is attractive even out of bloom.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Shuttleworth's wild ginger is more slowly spreading than Asarum canadense and has exceptionally attractive, silver-mottled, evergreen foliage.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Shuttleworth's wild ginger is slower-spreading than the common eastern wild ginger (A. canadense) but is evergreen and much more ornamental in foliage, making it superior for groundcover use in shaded gardens. Asarum shuttleworthii is a southern Appalachian native evergreen wild ginger with large, heart-shaped, leathery, dark-green leaves beautifully mottled and marbled with silvery patterns; the intricate mottling intensifies with the age of the plant and varies considerably between individual plants, making each one somewhat unique. Like all Asarum, it bears curious, jug-shaped, brownish-purple flowers concealed at the base of the leaves near the soil in spring; the leaves smell faintly of ginger when crushed and were used historically as a seasoning though the plant contains toxic compounds and is not recommended for internal use.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Canada plum is the most cold-hardy native North American plum and is invaluable for fruit production in zones 2–6 where other plums cannot survive.

directions: Plant grafted trees or bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Canada plum is the most cold-hardy of the native North American plums, surviving temperatures to -40°F and producing fruit reliably in zones 2–6 where most stone fruit trees would be killed by winter cold. Prunus nigra is a native small tree or large shrub of eastern and central Canada and the northern US producing prolific white flowers in early spring (before leaves) and medium-sized, oval plums ripening yellow to red to deep purple in late summer with a tart, wild-plum flavor excellent for jams, jellies, and sauce. Cross-pollinate with American plum (P. americana) or another Canada plum seedling for best fruit set; it also attracts wildlife heavily — the fruit is avidly eaten by bears, raccoons, foxes, and many bird species.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container shrubs in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Pacific ninebark is an excellent riparian shrub for stream banks, rain gardens, and moist native plant gardens on the West Coast.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container shrubs in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade along stream banks, pond margins, or in rain gardens; Pacific ninebark is a native riparian shrub of the Pacific Northwest excellent for stabilizing stream banks and moist slopes. Physocarpus capitatus is a large, vigorous, deciduous native shrub of western North American stream banks and moist slopes producing lobed, maple-like leaves, rounded clusters of small, creamy-white flowers in late spring, and ornamental, papery, red-bronze seed capsules in fall and winter; the exfoliating bark (which gives ninebark its name) peels in thin, multi-layered strips to reveal mahogany-colored inner bark, providing attractive winter texture. It is an important wildlife shrub, providing nesting cover, shelter, and browse for deer and elk; the flowers support native bees and butterflies. More vigorous and adapted to wet conditions than the eastern ninebark (P. opulifolius).
